1.exposition: Paul turns from the sovereignty of chapter 9 to the grief of a pastor. Before he presses the charge home, he clears himself of every suspicion of hatred: his heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved. What was wrong with Israel was not indifference but its opposite — a genuine zeal for God, only a zeal "not according to knowledge." Ignorant of the righteousness God gives and bent on establishing their own, they would not submit to God's righteousness. And the reason the law could never yield that righteousness is the fulcrum on which the whole chapter turns: Christ is the end of the law — its goal, and its termination as a way of standing before God.
1.original_language: The pivotal word is telos (v.4), and its sense is genuinely disputed. It can mean "termination, end" — the point at which a thing stops — or "goal, aim, consummation" — the point toward which it runs; the two readings pull in different directions, and the witnesses split. grammatical-historical Ellicott takes it strictly as termination: Christ "brings the functions of the law to an end by superseding it." grammatical-historical Robertson holds three senses at once — Christ put a stop to the law as a means of salvation, Christ is the goal or aim of the law (Galatians 3:24), and Christ is its fulfilment (Matthew 5:17). eastern Theodoret leans to goal and fulfilment: faith in Christ "is not contrary to the law," for "the law itself has directed us to the Lord Christ," so the believer "fulfils the intention of the law." Most read the phrase as holding both together: Christ is the destination the law always pointed to and the end of the law as a system for earning acceptance. The earlier phrase zēlos theou (v.2), "zeal for God," is an objective genitive — an earnestness aimed at God that has nonetheless outrun its knowledge.
1.historical_context: Paul writes as one who knew this zeal from the inside. "Zeal" (zēlos) was a prized virtue in Second-Temple Judaism, the fire of Phinehas (Numbers 25:11) and the Maccabees, the defense of Torah against pagan encroachment; Paul himself had persecuted the church out of exactly this zeal (Galatians 1:14; Philippians 3:6). The failure he diagnoses is therefore not irreligion but misdirected devotion. "Their own righteousness" (v.3) is not crude self-congratulation but the covenant standing Israel sought to secure by possessing and performing the law — the very thing chapter 9 said they pursued and missed (Romans 9:31).
1.christ: "Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes" (v.4) is the christological hinge of Paul's gospel. The law was never itself the righteousness; it was the pointer, the tutor leading to Christ (Galatians 3:24). In him the long line of types, sacrifices, and commands reaches its destination — as grammatical-historical Clarke puts it, "where the law ends, Christ begins." He does not abolish the law's demand — he fulfils it (Matthew 5:17) — but he does end the law as the mechanism by which sinners establish their standing before God. The goal of Israel's Torah-pursuit turns out to be a person; righteousness is not achieved but received.

verses.note: Before the rebuke, love. Paul opens with his heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel, that they may be saved. eastern Chrysostom sees the pastoral craft: about to reprove Israel "more vehemently than before," Paul first "does away with every suspicion of hatred," for one cannot at once desire, pray for, and hate the same people. eastern Theodoret hears "an ardent wish"; reformed Calvin insists the concern is real — "such a feeling arises only from genuine love." The severity that follows flows from this affection (cf. Romans 9:1–3).

Paul grants what few polemicists would: Israel has a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge. grammatical-historical Barnes, once such a zealot himself (Acts 26:5), bears honest witness to their earnestness; reformed Calvin notes they fell "only through ignorance, and not through malignancy of mind." wesleyan-arminian Wesley turns it on the reader with an epigram: "They had zeal without knowledge; we have knowledge without zeal." eastern Theodoret catches the tact — Paul "mingles his censure with praise, hiding as it were the hook beneath a bait."

The anatomy of the failure, in three strokes: ignorant of God's righteousness, seeking to establish their own, they did not submit. reformed Calvin fastens on the opposition — God's righteousness and man's "are opposed to one another, as things wholly contrary, and cannot stand together." grammatical-historical Robertson presses the verb: they "did not subject themselves" (ouch hypetagēsan) — a will unbowed, not merely a mind uninformed. grammatical-historical Barnes adds that the ignorance was "voluntary, and therefore criminal," since their own Scriptures pointed plainly to the Messiah (cf. John 5:39).

The verse the chapter turns on: Christ is the end (telos) of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes. Here the witnesses genuinely divide. grammatical-historical Ellicott reads telos as termination — Christ ends the law "by superseding it," so that the law "pursues a man until he takes refuge in Christ," then troubles him no more. grammatical-historical Robertson keeps three senses in play — a stop, a goal, a fulfilment. eastern Theodoret leans to goal: "he that trusts in Christ fulfils the intention of the law." reformed Gill draws the payoff — Christ being the law's end "for righteousness," the law can no longer be a way of justification for the one who believes.

witnesses.point: Paul removes every suspicion of hatred before rebuking Israel — one cannot desire, pray for, and hate the same people; the severity flows from love.
Israel fell through ignorance, not malignancy; God's righteousness and man's own are wholly contrary and cannot stand together.
Reads telos as termination — Christ brings the law's functions to an end by superseding it, the asylum where the law's pursuit finally stops.
Holds all three senses of telos together — Christ is the stop, the goal, and the fulfilment of the law.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: Surveying the options for telos, Moo concludes the primary sense is 'end/termination' — Christ terminates the law as a means of establishing righteousness — while granting that the law's forward-pointing role means Christ is also the goal it anticipated; the two are not finally separable.

1.application: The most dangerous religious condition is not coldness but sincere heat aimed at the wrong object. Zeal can be redirected and ignorance can be taught — but only where those who know God's righteousness in Christ speak it, as Paul does, with real love for the very people they must correct.

5.exposition: Two righteousnesses now stand side by side, each given a voice from Moses. The righteousness of the law speaks first and briefly: "the person who does them shall live by them" — do, and live. The righteousness of faith speaks longer and gentler, borrowing the words of Deuteronomy 30:12–14 to say salvation needs no impossible expedition: no ascent to heaven to fetch Christ down, no descent to the abyss to raise him up (vv.6–7), for the work is finished and the word is near — in your mouth and in your heart. That word is the confession that saves: Jesus is Lord, and God raised him from the dead. Heart and mouth together (v.10); and the promise is thrown open without distinction — "everyone who believes" (v.11), no difference between Jew and Greek (v.12), "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved".
5.original_language: Two focal points. First, Paul's use of Deuteronomy 30:14, "the word is near you." grammatical-historical Robertson notes this is "a free reproduction" of the Deuteronomy passage — Paul does not cite Moses as a proof-text but seizes his very words "with the orator's instinct" to voice the nearness of the gospel. In Deuteronomy the nearness is of the commandment; Paul hears in it the nearness of the rhēma tēs pisteōs, "the word of faith" — the proclaimed message (v.8). Second, the confession of v.9: Kyrios Iēsous, "Jesus is Lord." grammatical-historical Ellicott calls it "the earliest formal confession of faith" and denies any gap "between the outward confession and the inward act of faith" — the mouth's word is the heart's necessary expression. Then v.13 crowns it: Kyrios in Joel 2:32 is the covenant name of Israel's God, now the name on which men call to be saved in Christ.
5.historical_context: Paul's Deuteronomy reading is a christological midrash, not arbitrary fancy: Deuteronomy 30:11–14 stands in the great oracle of covenant renewal after exile, the promise that God will bring his word within reach; Paul hears it fulfilled in the gospel. The two "impossible" journeys — up to heaven, down to the abyss — map onto the incarnation and the resurrection, both already accomplished. And "Jesus is Lord" was a costly creed: in a Roman world where Kyrios was Caesar's own title, to confess Jesus as Lord was a political as well as a theological act — a confession that, as wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes, in a day of persecution "may send thee to the lions."
5.christ: The passage is dense with Christ. He is the one who "came down" (incarnation) and was "brought up" (resurrection), so that faith need not fetch him (vv.6–7). He is the Kyrios confessed, the risen one God vindicated (v.9). And in v.12 the christology reaches a height: "the same Lord is Lord of all." The witnesses even divide over whom "the same Lord" names — grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson hear Christ ("Christ is the Lord alike of Jew and of Gentile"), while reformed Calvin hears the Creator who "is the God of all men." Paul's next step settles it toward Christ: the promise of Joel 2:32 that "everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved" is applied to Jesus. eastern Cyril of Jerusalem draws the inference — the Lord proclaimed is "Himself the Lord," true God with the Father. To call on Jesus is to call on the name that saves.

verses.note: Moses describes the righteousness of the law: "the person who does them shall live by them" (Leviticus 18:5). reformed Gill notes Paul wisely argues "in the words of Moses," so esteemed among the Jews. wesleyan-arminian Wesley states the terms exactly: this life is for "he that perfectly keeps all these precepts in every point," and is therefore "impossible to any who have ever transgressed any one law in any point." eastern Theodoret agrees — perfect obedience wins life, but "any one transgression entails punishment." The law's own principle, do-and-live, shuts every sinner up.

The righteousness of faith speaks otherwise: "Do not say in your heart, Who will ascend into heaven?" — that is, to bring Christ down. grammatical-historical Robertson observes Paul "personifies" the faith-righteousness and lends it Moses's words from Deuteronomy 30:12. reformed Calvin admits the verse "may not a little disturb the reader," since Paul turns Moses's words to a fuller sense — yet the sense is true: Christ has already come, so faith need not scale heaven to find him. The incarnation is done.

"Or, Who will descend into the abyss?" — that is, to bring Christ up from the dead. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the words freely adapt Moses (Deuteronomy 30:13, "beyond the sea"), the sense unchanged: the thing is not remote and impossible. eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears the resurrection preached here — "to bring up Christ from the dead" recalls that "if Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain." Christ is risen; faith need not descend to raise him.

So the verdict: "The word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart" — that is, the word of faith we proclaim. grammatical-historical Barnes: what is near "may be easily obtained"; the doctrine "is not difficult to be understood and embraced." eastern Theodoret: the hearer needs "neither an ascent into heaven, nor a descent into Hades," for the knowledge is given freely. grammatical-historical Robertson hears the preacher's aim — "the living voice brings home to every one the faith kind of righteousness" over against the law.

The near word, spoken: if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved. reformed Gill describes a confession "sincere, and hearty," made "before the church... and before the world," arising "from a true faith in Christ in the heart." grammatical-historical Ellicott calls it the earliest Christian creed and denies any opposition between outward confession and inward faith. grammatical-historical Barnes: to confess is "to speak that which agrees with" what God has said of his Son. Resurrection-faith and Lord-confession are the two marks of the saved.

"With the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved." reformed Gill insists true faith lies "not in the brain, nor in the tongue, but in the heart," a believing "with all the heart." grammatical-historical Robertson notes the impersonal Greek ("it is believed... it is confessed") and that the true order is faith then confession — the heart the root, the mouth the fruit. eastern Theodoret: "both are necessary, a sound and firm faith, and a confession uttered with boldness."

"Everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame" (Isaiah 28:16, already cited at Romans 9:33). grammatical-historical Robertson marks the single word Paul adds to the prophet — pas, "everyone." reformed Calvin sees Paul, "having first hoisted an universal banner," now extending salvation expressly to the Gentiles. The believer's hope is never disappointed.

"No distinction between Jew and Greek; the same Lord is Lord of all, rich to all who call on him." puritan Matthew Henry: Jew and Gentile "stand upon the same level in point of acceptance with God." grammatical-historical Robertson glosses the riches as "the unsearchable riches of Christ" (Ephesians 3:8); reformed Calvin grounds the equality in the one Creator who "is the God of all men" and kind to all who call. The wall of distinction is down.

"Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved" (Joel 2:32, as Peter preached at Pentecost, Acts 2:21). grammatical-historical Barnes notes "to call on the name of the Lord is the same as to call on the Lord himself." grammatical-historical Ellicott observes the name meant Yahweh in Joel, and "here, therefore, it is applied to our Lord." eastern Cyril of Jerusalem draws the doctrine out — the Lord on whom we call "was with the Father," "Himself the Lord" — a quiet testimony to Christ's deity.

witnesses.point: Paul freely reproduces Deuteronomy 30, seizing Moses's words to preach the nearness of the word of faith; adds 'everyone' to Isaiah's promise.
Having hoisted an universal banner, Paul extends salvation to the Gentiles; grounds the 'same Lord' in the one Creator who is God of all who call.
The Lord on whom men call to be saved 'was with the Father' and is 'Himself the Lord' — the name of Yahweh in Joel applied to Christ testifies to his deity.
The law's 'do and live' is impossible to any who have ever transgressed; the confession of Christ may, in persecution, 'send thee to the lions.'

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: Wright reads Paul's use of Deuteronomy 30 not as arbitrary proof-texting but in its own setting of covenant renewal after exile, now fulfilled in the gospel word about the crucified and risen Messiah; and hears in 'the same Lord of all,' on whom Jew and Gentile alike call, Jesus drawn into the confession of Israel's one God.

5.application: The gospel's scandal is its nearness. It asks no pilgrimage and no heroics — only that you believe what God has already done and say so. Every attempt to make salvation more complicated than "Jesus is Lord, and God raised him" is an attempt to push back to a distance the word God has brought near.

14.pericope_label: All Who Call on the Lord Will Be Saved
14.exposition: If salvation is for everyone who calls (v.13), a chain of conditions runs backward from that call: no calling without believing, no believing without hearing, no hearing without a preacher, no preacher without a sending. The gospel is not stumbled upon; it is announced by messengers whose feet Isaiah calls beautiful. Yet the announcement is not everywhere obeyed — "Lord, who has believed our report?" So faith comes by hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ. Then Paul closes the case against Israel's excuse: they have heard (v.18) and they did know (v.19), for Moses and Isaiah foretold both the Gentiles' welcome and Israel's refusal (vv.19–20) — until the chapter ends on the ache of outstretched hands, held out all day long to a disobedient people.
14.original_language: A single Greek word ties the argument together: akoē means both the act of hearing and the thing heard, "the report." grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson note it stands behind both Isaiah's "who has believed our report?" (v.16) and Paul's "faith comes by hearing" (v.17) — so faith rests on a message heard, and that message is "the word of Christ" (rhēma Christou), which Robertson reads as an objective genitive: "the word about Christ."
14.historical_context: The citations are a tissue of Israel's own Scriptures reapplied. "Beautiful feet" comes from Isaiah 52:7, the herald announcing release from Babylon; Paul makes the gospel that herald's new-exodus good news. "Their voice has gone out to all the earth" (v.18) is Psalms 19:4, first sung of the wordless witness of the heavens, now the worldwide spread of the gospel. And the jealousy motif — "I will make you jealous of those who are not a nation" (v.19) — is Moses's own warning in Deuteronomy 32:21, which Paul will take up as God's very strategy for Israel's restoration in the next chapter (Romans 11:11). The Gentiles who "did not seek" (Isaiah 65:1) and disobedient Israel (Isaiah 65:2) are set, shockingly, side by side.
14.christ: "The word of Christ" (v.17) is the hinge — faith is created by the proclaimed Christ (his death, resurrection, and lordship) and directed toward him. The "beautiful feet" carry that word; the herald of Isaiah's new exodus becomes the gospel preacher, because the liberation Christ accomplished is the good news every messenger bears. And the chapter's tragic close is unmistakably his: the God who "all day long stretched out my hands to a disobedient people" (v.21) is the same God who, in Christ, would stretch out his hands upon a cross over a city he longed to gather (Matthew 23:37). The rejected pleading of the Father is taken up in the pierced hands of the Son.

verses.note: "How shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? and how believe in him of whom they have not heard? and how hear without a preacher?" eastern Chrysostom sees the purpose: having granted Israel's zeal and ignorance, Paul now "shows that for this ignorance itself they were punishable," building the argument "out of questions and answers" that leave no excuse. reformed Calvin reads the "gradation" as vindicating the Gentile mission — Paul's own calling to preach where Christ was not yet named.

"And how shall they preach unless they are sent?" — then Isaiah on the messengers: "How beautiful are the feet of those who preach good news!" reformed Gill notes there is "no proper... preaching of the word without a mission," whether extraordinary (the apostles') or ordinary (pastors sent by Christ through the church). grammatical-historical Robertson hears in Isaiah's picture "the messengers of the restoration from the Jewish captivity," now the gospel's own heralds. reformed Calvin: the gift of a preacher is "a proof and a pledge of divine love" toward a nation.

"But they have not all obeyed the gospel" — for Isaiah asks, "Lord, who has believed our report?" reformed Gill: though ministers "rightly preach the gospel," there is "no success without the power of God attending it"; men may hear and yet not obey. grammatical-historical Robertson puts it bluntly — "they heard, but did not heed." eastern Theodoret notes Scripture "was not silent on this point," having foretold the unbelief long before it came.

"So faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of Christ." reformed Gill: preaching "is the means God makes use of, to convey faith into the hearts of his people," ministers being "the instruments by whom others believe." grammatical-historical Robertson glosses "the word of Christ" as "the word about Christ" (objective genitive). wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds the careful qualifier — faith "ordinarily cometh by hearing... the word of God" — the appointed means, though God is not bound by it.

"Have they not heard? Indeed they have: their voice has gone out to all the earth" (Psalms 19:4). grammatical-historical Barnes: Paul does not deny the principle but affirms the fact — the gospel had actually been proclaimed. grammatical-historical Robertson catches the Greek's force — "did they fail to hear?" expecting a firm no. The wordless witness of the heavens becomes an image of the gospel's reach, and the unbelieving are left without excuse.

"Did Israel not know? First Moses says, 'I will make you jealous of those who are not a nation'" (Deuteronomy 32:21). reformed Gill: Israel did know — "the oracles of God" were theirs — so their ignorance was culpable. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the sting: Israel had worshipped "no-gods," and now God shows favor to a "no-nation." puritan Matthew Henry marvels that God would "make himself known to us when we did not ask after him."

"Isaiah is very bold: 'I was found by those who did not seek me'" (Isaiah 65:1). reformed Gill: the prophet "did not mince the matter," declaring the Gentiles' call "with great courage," though it risked "his credit and even his life" among the Jews. grammatical-historical Barnes: Isaiah spoke "openly, boldly, without any reserve." grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the words first meant apostate Israel; Paul "here applies it to the Gentiles" — the sovereign grace that finds the unseeking.

"But to Israel he says, 'All day long I have stretched out my hands to a disobedient and contrary people'" (Isaiah 65:2). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson render the bold metaphor — God "spread out" his hands to "a people disobeying and talking back," "all day long" meaning "without ceasing." grammatical-historical Clarke hears God "manifesting the utmost readiness... to gather them all," his hands held out in vain. The chapter closes on divine patience met by human refusal — the tragedy the next chapter will not leave as the last word.

witnesses.point: The chain of questions takes from Israel all excuse — for their very ignorance they were punishable, since the gospel was preached and could be heard, believed, and called upon.
No preaching without a lawful mission; and no success in preaching without the power of God — men may hear the rightly-preached word and still not obey it.
'They heard, but did not heed'; faith comes by the word about Christ (objective genitive); Isaiah's herald pictures the restoration now fulfilled in gospel messengers.
Moses and Isaiah foretold the Gentiles' call and Israel's refusal — God makes himself known to those who never asked after him, and stretches out his hands all day to those who do.

external.name: Thomas R. Schreiner
external.point: Schreiner reads the chain of questions as establishing preaching as the God-appointed means of salvation — faith is awakened by the proclaimed word about Christ, never self-generated — while insisting that Israel's failure is culpable unbelief, not lack of opportunity: they heard and understood, yet refused to obey.

14.application: "Faith comes by hearing" makes the church's proclamation non-negotiable and its silence costly: no one believes a Christ they have never heard. Yet the chapter's end is sobering — proclamation is not the same as persuasion, and the messenger's fruit rests on the power of God. We preach, and we pray for the hands God is still holding out.

_meta.title: The Word Is Near You
reflection: Paul grants that Israel has "a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge" (Romans 10:2). Where might your own religious earnestness be aimed slightly off its true object — and what would it cost to let it be corrected?

"The word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart" (Romans 10:8). Salvation asks no heroic ascent or descent, only that you believe what God has done and confess it. Do you tend to make the gospel harder — more conditional, more performance-bound — than Paul makes it here?

"How are they to believe in him of whom they have never heard? And how are they to hear without someone preaching?" (Romans 10:14). If faith comes by hearing, what does that lay on you for the people around you who have not yet heard?
